retrieved_contexts: [{"text": "The LLM-as-a-judge approach uses a large language model to evaluate the outputs of another LLM or AI system. This approach scales evaluation beyond what manual review can handle but introduces its own biases including verbosity bias, position bias, and self-enhancement bias.", "filename": "_articles_llm-as-a-judge_annotated.docx"}]

case_description: D2: prerequisite only — what evaluation biases are vs how to calibrate
question: How do I calibrate my LLM-as-a-judge evaluator against human annotations to reduce scoring bias?
answer: You collect a set of human-annotated examples, run your LLM judge on the same inputs, and adjust your prompt or scoring rubric to minimize divergence.
retrieved_contexts: [{"text": "The LLM-as-a-judge approach introduces several known biases that evaluator designers must account for. Verbosity bias causes judges to prefer longer responses even when shorter ones are more accurate. Position bias leads judges to favor responses presented first in a comparison. Self-enhancement bias occurs when an LLM rates its own outputs higher than those from other models. These biases can compound in production and lead to systematically skewed quality assessments.", "filename": "_articles_llm-as-a-judge_annotated.docx"}]
